reasoning: Wdr48 (also called UAF1) functions mainly as a regulatory co‑factor for several ubiquitin‑specific proteases (USP1, USP12, USP46). Its best‑characterized roles are in the DNA damage response, replication fork stability and cell‑cycle control through de‑ubiquitination of substrates such as PCNA and FANCD2. There is no well‑established signaling pathway that links Wdr48 activity directly to the transcriptional regulation of the chemokine Ccl2 (MCP‑1). Ccl2 expression is primarily driven by inflammatory cues (e.g., NF‑κB, MAPK, cytokines) rather than by the ubiquitin‑dependent DNA‑repair machinery in which Wdr48 participates. Consequently, knocking down Wdr48 with CRISPRi is unlikely to produce a consistent, direct up‑ or down‑regulation of Ccl2; any effect would be indirect and context‑dependent. Therefore the most plausible outcome is no specific change in Ccl2 expression.
